text: This video is a presentation of a unique transgastric resection of a leiomyoma located just distal to the esophagogastric junction. Surgery was performed at Alta Bates Summit Medical Center in Oakland, California. We have no financial relationships. The patient is a forty-three-year-old male with a history of testicular cancer who underwent a surveillance CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis. The imaging revealed a new three point five centimeter mass located at the GE junction and he was referred for endoscopic ultrasound. Biopsy was performed at that time and demonstrated spindle-like cells consistent with leiomyoma. Again, a CT demonstrated a mass at the GE junction indicated by the arrow. Note that it is well circumscribed and appears to be submucosal.

The video shows the initial port placement for the procedure, including three working robotic ports and a twelve millimeter assistant port. The camera port and one robotic port are repositioned more superiorly to improve intragastric views and instrumentation. Laparoscopy reveals no peritoneal disease, while endoscopy confirms the presence of the mass at the gastroesophageal junction.

The final port placement in the stomach is shown, with the camera port flanked by two working arms. Electrocautery scoring of the serosa is visible, and enterocars are placed under direct laparoscopic and endoscopic visualization. The z-line of the gastroesophageal junction is clearly demonstrated, located immediately adjacent to the visible mass.

The surgeon makes a longitudinal mucosal incision along the mass, carefully avoiding the transitional mucosa. Dissection continues in the submucosal plane, revealing the well-encapsulated mass. The submucosal cavity is inspected for hemostasis, and the mucosal incision is closed with a running absorbable suture, ensuring the z-line is not included in the closure.

The camera is repositioned to the inferior port in the stomach, and the specimen is extracted from the previous camera port using a standard specimen bag. The gastrotomies along the greater curvature of the stomach are closed with interrupted absorbable sutures. A leak test is conducted to ensure the closure is airtight.

The patient was observed overnight in the hospital and was discharged home on postoperative day one tolerating a regular diet. The final pathology confirmed a benign leiomyoma. He benefited from the robotic approach in this case for several reasons. Endoscopic resection, though acceptable for submucosal masses, would not have been feasible due to the size of the mass. Furthermore, a purely laparoscopic or robotic approach would require a wedge resection of the mass. Because of its location at the GE junction, this would also not be feasible. Instead, he would have undergone a partial esophageal resection and anastomosis, which is fraught with potential complications. Finally, the local resection allowed this patient to eat regularly sooner and return home in less than twenty-four hours.
